transcript.argument: Courage is not recklessness — the Aristotelian mean requires discernment about what risks are proportionate and meaningful. Playing through an injury that will require surgery and end your season entirely is not courageous; it crosses into the excess of recklessness. True courage would mean facing the harder social cost: enduring criticism from teammates and fans, accepting the painful outcome of sitting out, and resisting the emotional pull to sacrifice long-term wellbeing for a single match. The genuinely fearful thing to face here is not physical pain on the field but rather the disappointment, judgment, and sense of letting others down — and courage demands you bear that cost with integrity rather than fleeing it through a self-destructive heroic gesture.

Temperance counsels proportionate response to appetite and impulse — including the powerful impulse of competitive desire, team loyalty, and the intoxicating pull of a decisive moment. Playing through a worsening injury is an act of excess: sacrificing long-term bodily integrity for short-term emotional satisfaction. The temperate person resists the inflamed passion of the moment and asks what is proportionate across the full arc of life, not just this match. Sitting out is not insensibility or cowardice — it is the measured, restrained choice that preserves the capacity to act well over time. Moderation here means accepting a painful loss now rather than indulging an immoderate sacrifice of one's health and future career.

From the perspective of justice, each party is owed their due: the player is owed fair treatment of their own legitimate interests, the team is owed honest contribution, and the sport itself demands integrity. Playing through a serious injury may appear self-sacrificial, but it distorts what the player legitimately owes the team — their healthy, capable performance over time — not a one-time gamble with their bodily welfare. Justice also concerns respecting legitimate rules and roles: medical staff, club contracts, and sporting regulations exist precisely to protect players from coercive pressure to play injured, and honoring those frameworks is a matter of fair dealing. Furthermore, forcing oneself into action under duress that permanently harms one's career denies the player what is rightfully theirs — a fair opportunity to contribute in future seasons — making Action A a net injustice to the player themselves. Sitting out (B) respects the fair distribution of burdens and benefits across time, preserving the player's capacity to fulfill their duties justly going forward.

Prudence demands we weigh not just immediate consequences but the full arc of consequences across time. Playing through a worsening injury trades a single match victory for surgery, months of rehabilitation, and jeopardized long-term career prospects — a deeply unfavorable exchange when assessed practically. The prudent person distinguishes between short-term team benefit and the sustainable good of the athlete's continued contribution across many future seasons. Furthermore, a player who permanently damages their health provides far less value to the team over time than one who recovers and plays well for years; the means (risking serious injury) do not actually serve the deeper end (being a valuable team contributor). The wise choice is to protect the capacity for future good performance rather than gamble it on one elimination match.

Honesty demands I be truthful about the actual condition of my body and the real risks involved — concealing or minimizing the severity of an injury to appear strong or selfless would be a form of self-deception and deception toward the team. If I know playing will likely cause serious long-term harm, then choosing to play while presenting it as a noble sacrifice obscures the truth: I am gambling with my future capacity, not just enduring temporary pain. Transparency with coaches, teammates, and medical staff about my true physical state is the honest course, and that honest assessment points toward sitting out. Pretending the risk is acceptable when I know it isn't would be a kind of boastful self-sacrifice — performing heroism at the cost of honesty about real consequences.

True generosity means giving the right things to the right people at the right time — not reckless self-sacrifice. Playing through a serious injury gives the team one match at the cost of potentially giving nothing to anyone for months or years. Sitting out preserves my capacity to give generously to teammates, coaches, and fans across a full future season. A prodigal gift of one's entire health for a single game is not generous — it is wasteful excess. The genuinely generous act is stewarding my ability to contribute so that I can keep giving sustainably.

Humility requires accurate self-assessment, and that includes honest appraisal of one's own physical condition and limitations. A humble agent resists the inflated self-narrative that says 'only I can save the team' — that kind of thinking overstates one's indispensability and underestimates teammates. Furthermore, humility means acknowledging uncertainty: the belief that playing guarantees a win is overconfident, while the harm to one's body is more certain. Sitting out is not cowardice or self-effacement; it is clear-eyed recognition of one's actual situation rather than a heroic story one tells oneself under social pressure.

Honesty requires accurate disclosure about one's true physical condition — not minimizing injury severity to perform heroism, and not concealing the real stakes from coaches and medical staff. If I know playing will cause serious long-term damage, presenting that choice as mere 'pushing through pain' is a distortion of reality. The honest agent names the facts plainly: surgery and career jeopardy are not acceptable prices to obscure behind the language of sacrifice. Transparency about my actual state points unambiguously toward sitting out, and the courage agent's observation that the harder honest act is bearing criticism rather than fleeing to a self-destructive gesture reinforces this — the boastful excess would be performing heroism while hiding the truth of what I'm actually giving up.

Temperance sees clearly the excess at work in Action A: the inflamed competitive impulse, the intoxicating pull of being the decisive hero, the social pressure that inflates the moment beyond its actual proportion. These are precisely the appetites and passions that temperance is charged with moderating. The temperate person asks not 'what does this moment demand of me emotionally?' but 'what is the proportionate response across the full arc of my life and capacity?' Sacrificing surgery-level bodily harm and months of rehabilitation for a single match is categorically disproportionate — an indulgence of passion masquerading as virtue. Sitting out is the restrained, measured choice that preserves the ability to act well over time, which is the very definition of moderation.

True generosity means giving the right things to the right people at the right time — not reckless self-sacrifice that depletes the very capacity to give. Playing through an injury that will require surgery and months of rehabilitation is prodigality in the worst sense: burning through a resource (my health and future availability) to make a single dramatic gift that forecloses countless future contributions. The generous agent notices that teammates and fans who receive this one match also lose me for the rest of the season and potentially beyond — that is not generous, it is wasteful. Sitting out preserves my ability to keep giving: to teammates in future seasons, to coaches who need a healthy contributor, to the fans who benefit from sustained excellence. Stewardship of one's capacity to give is itself a form of generosity.

Justice requires giving each party their due, and here the player is owed fair consideration of their own legitimate interests — not merely as an instrument for a single team outcome. The player owes the team their best sustained contribution over time, not a one-time self-destructive gesture that strips them of future seasons. Sporting institutions, medical frameworks, and contracts exist precisely to protect players from coercive pressure to play injured, and honoring those structures is a matter of fair dealing and rule-respect. Action A denies the player what is rightfully theirs — a fair opportunity to contribute in future seasons — constituting a net injustice to themselves. Sitting out distributes burdens and benefits fairly across time, preserving the capacity to fulfill duties justly going forward.

I hold my position from round 0. Courage is the mean between cowardice and recklessness, and Action A clearly falls on the reckless side — trading surgery, rehabilitation, and jeopardized career for one match. The genuinely courageous act is bearing the harder social cost: absorbing criticism, accepting the team's disappointment, and resisting the seductive pull of performing heroism in a moment of high emotion. Nothing in round 1 has given me reason to revise; the council's convergence on B is not social pressure but independent confirmation that Action A fails the virtue test from multiple angles, which strengthens rather than moves my stance.

Prudence remains firm on B. The practical calculus is clear: surgery, months of rehabilitation, and jeopardized long-term career prospects are not proportionate costs for a single elimination match victory. The prudent person traces consequences forward — a player who destroys their health provides far less to the team across future seasons than one who recovers and contributes sustainably. I note the humility agent's point that the belief 'only I can save this match' is itself a form of overconfidence; the harm to one's body is certain while the win is only probable, making the risk-reward ratio even worse upon closer examination. The means chosen must actually serve the deeper end: being a genuine, lasting contributor to the team — and Action A undermines that end entirely.

Humility requires accurate self-assessment, and the most dangerous distortion here is the narrative of indispensability — the belief that 'only I can save the team' inflates one's importance and underestimates teammates' capacity to compete. A humble agent also recognizes the asymmetry of certainty: the harm to one's body is known and serious, while the win is merely probable. I notice the honesty agent's reinforcement that presenting self-destructive sacrifice as heroism involves a kind of self-deception, which aligns with humility's demand that we see ourselves clearly rather than through a flattering narrative of sacrifice. Sitting out is not self-effacement — it is honest recognition of one's actual situation, limits, and the realistic assessment that long-term contribution matters more than one dramatic moment.

Temperance sees in Action A the classic shape of excess: appetite and impulse — competitive desire, the thrill of the decisive moment, the social pull of being the hero — overwhelming rational proportion. The temperate person does not ask 'what does this emotionally charged moment demand?' but 'what is proportionate across the full arc of my life and capacity?' Surgery, months of rehabilitation, and jeopardized career are categorically disproportionate costs for one match; that is not restraint, it is indulgence of passion dressed as virtue. Sitting out is the measured, restrained choice — neither the insensibility of never caring about the team, nor the self-indulgent excess of burning one's health and future on a single inflamed moment. The council's independent convergence on B from multiple angles confirms rather than pressures my position, and nothing in the deliberation has given temperance a reason to reconsider.

Courage remains steady at B through round 2. The Aristotelian mean requires that risk-taking be proportionate to what is genuinely at stake, and Action A fails that test categorically: surgery, months of rehabilitation, and a jeopardized career are not proportionate costs for one elimination match. The reckless person mistakes self-destruction for bravery; the truly courageous act here is bearing the harder, less glamorous cost — absorbing criticism, sitting with the team's disappointment, and resisting the powerful emotional pull to perform heroism in a high-stakes moment. Nothing in round 2 has moved me; the council's convergence on B reflects independent virtue reasoning, which confirms rather than pressures my stance.

Honesty requires accurate disclosure of one's true physical condition to coaches, medical staff, and teammates — not a sanitized narrative that frames serious long-term harm as mere 'pushing through pain.' If I know that playing will likely result in surgery and career jeopardy, presenting that choice as heroic sacrifice obscures the actual facts: I would be concealing the real stakes from those who have a legitimate interest in knowing them. The boastful excess that honesty guards against is precisely this kind of performed self-sacrifice that hides the truth of what is actually being wagered. Transparency about my genuine physical state points unambiguously toward sitting out, and nothing in this round's deliberation has given me a reason grounded in truthfulness to revise that assessment.

True generosity means giving the right things to the right people at the right time — not prodigal self-sacrifice that destroys the very capacity to give. Playing through an injury that demands surgery and months of rehabilitation is wasteful excess: I burn through a finite resource (my health and continued availability) to make one dramatic gift that forecloses countless future contributions to teammates, coaches, and fans. The genuinely generous act is stewardship — preserving my ability to keep giving across a full future season rather than spending it all in one moment. A gift that leaves the recipient with nothing from me for the rest of the season and potentially beyond is not generous; it is reckless, and recklessness is the excess my virtue is defined against.

Humility requires accurate self-assessment rather than a flattering self-narrative, and the most seductive distortion here is the belief in one's own indispensability — 'only I can save this team' is a claim that overstates one's importance and underestimates teammates. A humble agent also notices the asymmetry of certainty: the bodily harm is known and serious, while the win is merely probable, making the heroic calculus far less compelling under honest scrutiny. Nothing in round 2 has given me new reason to revise; the council's independent convergence on B from multiple virtue perspectives confirms rather than pressures my assessment. Sitting out is not self-effacement but clear-eyed recognition of one's actual limits and the realistic judgment that sustained future contribution outweighs one dramatic moment.

Justice requires giving each party their due, and that includes the player themselves — their legitimate interest in bodily integrity and future career opportunity is not negated by team loyalty. The player owes the team their best sustained contribution over time, not a single self-destructive gesture that strips them of future seasons of service. Sporting institutions, medical frameworks, and contracts exist precisely to protect players from coercive pressure to play injured; honoring those legitimate structures is itself a matter of fair dealing and rule-respect. Action A constitutes a net injustice to the player by denying them what is rightfully theirs — fair opportunity to contribute in future seasons — while Action B distributes burdens and benefits proportionately across time, preserving the capacity to fulfill duties justly going forward.

Prudence remains firmly on B. The practical calculus is unambiguous: trading surgery, months of rehabilitation, and jeopardized long-term career prospects for a single elimination match victory is a deeply unfavorable exchange by any reasonable measure of consequences. The prudent person traces effects forward across the full arc of time — a player who destroys their health provides far less value to the team over many future seasons than one who recovers and contributes sustainably. The humility agent's observation that the belief 'only I can save this match' is itself overconfidence sharpens the prudential case: the bodily harm is certain while the win is merely probable, making the risk-reward ratio worse still upon clear-eyed examination. The means chosen — risking serious injury — actively undermine the deeper end: being a genuine, lasting contributor to the team.

judge.outcome_reasoning: The council unanimously chose B and reframed courage as recklessness, but the genuine virtue cost of B (team elimination, breaking commitment, letting others down) was mostly dismissed rather than seriously weighed; the courage side's strongest case for sacrifice was never given a real advocate.
judge.fidelity_notes: Each agent stayed distinctly within its virtue's conceptual vocabulary (mean/recklessness for courage, proportion/appetite for temperance, due/rule-respect for justice, stewardship for generosity, self-assessment for humility). No agent collapsed into generic moralizing.
